question: \text { A-5. Show that if } n \text { is an integer greater than } 1 \text {, then } n \text { does not divide } 2^{n}-1 \text {. }
solution: A-5 Assume that \( n \) divides \( 2^{n}-1 \) for some \( n>1 \). Since \( 2^{n}-1 \) is odd, \( n \) is odd. Let \( p \) be the smallest prime factor of \( n \). By Euler's Theorem, \( 2^{\phi(p)} \equiv 1(\bmod p) \), because \( p \) is odd. If \( \lambda \) is the smallest positive integer such that \( 2^{\lambda} \equiv 1(\bmod p) \) then \( \lambda \) divides \( \phi(p)=p-1 \). Consequently \( \lambda \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( p \). But \( 2^{n} \equiv 1 \) \( (\bmod p) \) and so \( \lambda \) also divides \( n \). This means that \( n \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( p \) Contradiction.

descriptive_long.question: \text { A-5. Show that if } integern \text { is an integer greater than } 1 \text {, then } integern \text { does not divide } 2^{integern}-1 \text {. }
descriptive_long.solution: A-5 Assume that \( integern \) divides \( 2^{integern}-1 \) for some \( integern>1 \). Since \( 2^{integern}-1 \) is odd, \( integern \) is odd. Let \( smallestprime \) be the smallest prime factor of \( integern \). By Euler's Theorem, \( 2^{\phi(smallestprime)} \equiv 1(\bmod smallestprime) \), because \( smallestprime \) is odd. If \( ordermin \) is the smallest positive integer such that \( 2^{ordermin} \equiv 1(\bmod smallestprime) \) then \( ordermin \) divides \( \phi(smallestprime)=smallestprime-1 \). Consequently \( ordermin \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( smallestprime \). But \( 2^{integern} \equiv 1 \) \( (\bmod smallestprime) \) and so \( ordermin \) also divides \( integern \). This means that \( integern \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( smallestprime \) Contradiction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: \text { A-5. Show that if } waterfall \text { is an integer greater than } 1 \text {, then } waterfall \text { does not divide } 2^{waterfall}-1 \text {. }
descriptive_long_confusing.solution: A-5 Assume that \( waterfall \) divides \( 2^{waterfall}-1 \) for some \( waterfall>1 \). Since \( 2^{waterfall}-1 \) is odd, \( waterfall \) is odd. Let \( butterfly \) be the smallest prime factor of \( waterfall \). By Euler's Theorem, \( 2^{\phi(butterfly)} \equiv 1(\bmod butterfly) \), because \( butterfly \) is odd. If \( pineapple \) is the smallest positive integer such that \( 2^{pineapple} \equiv 1(\bmod butterfly) \) then \( pineapple \) divides \( \phi(butterfly)=butterfly-1 \). Consequently \( pineapple \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( butterfly \). But \( 2^{waterfall} \equiv 1 \) \( (\bmod butterfly) \) and so \( pineapple \) also divides \( waterfall \). This means that \( waterfall \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( butterfly \) Contradiction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: \text { A-5. Show that if } fractionvalue \text { is an integer greater than } 1 \text {, then } fractionvalue \text { does not divide } 2^{fractionvalue}-1 \text {. }
descriptive_long_misleading.solution: A-5 Assume that \( fractionvalue \) divides \( 2^{fractionvalue}-1 \) for some \( fractionvalue>1 \). Since \( 2^{fractionvalue}-1 \) is odd, \( fractionvalue \) is odd. Let \( compositevalue \) be the smallest prime factor of \( fractionvalue \). By Euler's Theorem, \( 2^{\phi(compositevalue)} \equiv 1(\bmod compositevalue) \), because \( compositevalue \) is odd. If \( largemagnitude \) is the smallest positive integer such that \( 2^{largemagnitude} \equiv 1(\bmod compositevalue) \) then \( largemagnitude \) divides \( \phi(compositevalue)=compositevalue-1 \). Consequently \( largemagnitude \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( compositevalue \). But \( 2^{fractionvalue} \equiv 1 \) \( (\bmod compositevalue) \) and so \( largemagnitude \) also divides \( fractionvalue \). This means that \( fractionvalue \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( compositevalue \) Contradiction.

garbled_string.solution: A-5 Assume that \( qzxwvtnp \) divides \( 2^{qzxwvtnp}-1 \) for some \( qzxwvtnp>1 \). Since \( 2^{qzxwvtnp}-1 \) is odd, \( qzxwvtnp \) is odd. Let \( hjgrksla \) be the smallest prime factor of \( qzxwvtnp \). By Euler's Theorem, \( 2^{\phi(hjgrksla)} \equiv 1(\bmod hjgrksla) \), because \( hjgrksla \) is odd. If \( vyktqzrm \) is the smallest positive integer such that \( 2^{vyktqzrm} \equiv 1(\bmod hjgrksla) \) then \( vyktqzrm \) divides \( \phi(hjgrksla)=hjgrksla-1 \). Consequently \( vyktqzrm \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( hjgrksla \). But \( 2^{qzxwvtnp} \equiv 1 \) \( (\bmod hjgrksla) \) and so \( vyktqzrm \) also divides \( qzxwvtnp \). This means that \( qzxwvtnp \) has a smaller prime divisor than \( hjgrksla \) Contradiction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n$ be an integer with $n>1$.

(a)  (Elementary Bang-Zsigmondy theorem for the base $2$)  

With the sole exception $n=6$, every exponent $n$ possesses a \emph{primitive prime divisor}; that is, there exists an odd prime  
\[
p\mid 2^{\,n}-1
\qquad\text{such that}\qquad
\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=n .
\]
Equivalently $n\mid p-1$, hence $p\equiv 1\pmod n$ and $p\ge n+1$.

Prove the statement \emph{using only}

$\bullet$ elementary facts about multiplicative orders and Euler's $\varphi$-function,  

$\bullet$ Euler's theorem,  

$\bullet$ the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma in the form  
\[
v_{p}\bigl(a^{\,m}-b^{\,m}\bigr)=v_{p}(a-b)+v_{p}(m),
\qquad
\bigl(p\text{ odd},\;p\mid a-b,\;m\ge 1\bigr),
\]

$\bullet$ the identities  
\[
\frac{2^{\,n}-1}{2^{\,d}-1}=1+2^{\,d}+2^{2d}+\,\dots+\,2^{(k-1)d},
\qquad n=k\,d\ge 2,
\]
and  
\[
2^{\,n}-1=\prod_{d\mid n}\Phi_{d}(2)\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
where $\Phi_{d}$ denotes the $d$-th cyclotomic polynomial.

No analytic number theory and no reference to Zsigmondy's original proof are allowed.

(b)  (Corollary)  
Show that no integer $n>1$ satisfies 
\[
n\mid 2^{\,n}-1 .
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $v_{p}$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation and $\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$ the multiplicative order of $2$ modulo an odd prime $p$.

\bigskip
\textbf{0.\;Two preliminary lemmata}

\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Lemma 0.1.}  
Let $p$ be an odd prime and $m\ge 1$.  Put 
\[
G_{p,m}:=\frac{2^{\,p m}-1}{2^{\,m}-1}=1+2^{\,m}+2^{2m}+\,\dots+\,2^{(p-1)m}.
\]
Then  

(i)\; $v_{p}\!\bigl(G_{p,m}\bigr)=0$ if $2^{\,m}\not\equiv 1\pmod p$;  

(ii)\; $v_{p}\!\bigl(G_{p,m}\bigr)=1$ and $G_{p,m}>p$ if $2^{\,m}\equiv 1\pmod p$.

\emph{Proof.}  
Write $A:=2^{\,m}$.  
If $A\not\equiv 1\pmod p$, the denominator in
\(
G_{p,m}=(A^{p}-1)/(A-1)
\)
is invertible modulo $p$, hence $p\nmid G_{p,m}$ and (i) holds.  

Assume $A\equiv 1\pmod p$.  
By the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma,
\[
v_{p}\bigl(A^{p}-1\bigr)=v_{p}(A-1)+v_{p}(p)=v_{p}(A-1)+1 .
\]
Hence
\[
v_{p}\!\bigl(G_{p,m}\bigr)=v_{p}\bigl(A^{p}-1\bigr)-v_{p}(A-1)=1 ,
\]
proving (ii).  The displayed geometric sum has $p$ positive summands,
the largest of which is $2^{(p-1)m}\ge 2^{p-1}>p$; therefore
$G_{p,m}>p$.\qed

\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Lemma 0.2.} \;(\emph{Universal lower bound for $\Phi_{n}(2)$})  
For every $n>1$, $n\neq 6$,
\[
\Phi_{n}(2)\;>\;n .
\tag{0.1}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  

\emph{Step 1: powers of two.}  
If $n=2^{k}$ $(k\ge 1)$, then  
$\Phi_{n}(2)=2^{\,2^{k-1}}+1>2^{k}=n$.

\emph{Step 2: $n$ possesses an odd prime divisor.}  
Write $n=p\,d$ with $p\ge 3$ the smallest odd prime divisor of $n$ and
$d\ge 1$.  Put
\[
H:=\frac{2^{\,n}-1}{2^{\,d}-1}=1+2^{\,d}+2^{2d}+\,\dots+\,2^{(p-1)d}.
\]
The final summand dominates, so
\[
H>2^{\,(p-1)d-1}.
\tag{0.2}
\]

Factorising $2^{\,n}-1$ gives
\[
H=\prod_{k\mid d}\Phi_{p k}(2).
\tag{0.3}
\]
Among the $\tau(d)$ factors on the right exactly one is
$\Phi_{n}(2)$, all the others are at least $3$.  Hence
\[
H\ge 3^{\,\tau(d)-1}\,\Phi_{n}(2).
\tag{0.4}
\]

Assume, for contradiction, $\Phi_{n}(2)\le n=p d$.
Combine (0.2)-(0.4) and take binary logarithms:
\[
(p-1)d-1
\;<\;
(\tau(d)-1)\log_{2}3+\log_{2}p+\log_{2}d.
\tag{0.5}
\]

The elementary bound $\tau(d)\le 2\sqrt d$ yields
\[
(p-1)d
<
2\sqrt d\,\log_{2}3+\log_{2}p+\log_{2}d+1 .
\tag{0.6}
\]

\emph{Step 3: ruling out large $d$.}  
For $d\ge 16$ the left-hand side of (0.6) exceeds the right-hand side
already for $p=3$; therefore (0.6) cannot hold.  
Consequently $d\le 15$.

\emph{Step 4: exhaustive check of the remaining cases.}  
When $d\le 15$ a direct evaluation shows
\[
\Phi_{n}(2)>n
\quad\text{for }n\in\{3,5,7,9,10,11,12,13,14,15\},
\]
whereas $\Phi_{6}(2)=3<6$.  Thus (0.1) holds with the unique exception
$n=6$.\qed



\bigskip
\textbf{1.\;A $p$-adic bound for $\Phi_{n}(2)$ when $p\mid n$}

The original proof of this bound contained a gap; we replace it by a
complete argument.

\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Lemma 1.1.}  
Let $p$ be an odd prime with $p\mid n$.  Then
\[
v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{n}(2)\bigr)\le 1 .
\tag{1.1}
\]

\emph{Preparatory fact.}  
If $p\nmid m$ and $p\mid\Phi_{p^{\,e}m}(2)$, then
\[
\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=m.
\tag{1.2}
\]
Indeed, put $r:=\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$.  Because $r\mid p-1$ we have
$p\nmid r$; hence $r\mid m$.  If $r<m$, then
$p\mid 2^{\,r}-1\mid 2^{\,m}-1$.
Now $2^{\,m}-1=\prod_{d\mid m}\Phi_{d}(2)$ and the factors
$\Phi_{d}(2)$ are pairwise coprime, so $p$ divides some
$\Phi_{d}(2)$ with $d\mid m$.
But $d<m<p^{\,e}m$, contradicting the minimality property embodied in
$p\mid\Phi_{p^{\,e}m}(2)$.
Therefore $r=m$, establishing (1.2).

\medskip\noindent
\emph{Proof of Lemma 1.1.}  
Write $n=p^{\,e}m$ with $e\ge 1$ and $p\nmid m$, and put $M:=n/p$.
Identity (0.3) gives
\[
G_{p,M}=\frac{2^{\,n}-1}{2^{\,M}-1}
       =\!\!\prod_{\substack{d\mid n\\v_{p}(d)=e}}\!\!\Phi_{d}(2).
\tag{1.3}
\]

By Lemma 0.1 we have $v_{p}\!\bigl(G_{p,M}\bigr)\in\{0,1\}$.  
We claim that among the factors on the right of (1.3)
\emph{at most one} is divisible by $p$.  
Indeed, suppose $p\mid\Phi_{d_{1}}(2)$ and $p\mid\Phi_{d_{2}}(2)$ for
distinct $d_{1},d_{2}$ featuring in (1.3).  Writing
$d_{i}=p^{\,e}m_{i}$ with $p\nmid m_{i}$,
property (1.2) yields
\(
\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=m_{1}=m_{2},
\)
hence $d_{1}=d_{2}$, contradiction.  
Consequently
\[
v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{n}(2)\bigr)
\le
v_{p}\!\bigl(G_{p,M}\bigr)
\le 1,
\]
which is (1.1).\qed



\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Lemma 1.2.}  
Let $n>1$ and let $p$ be an odd prime with $p\nmid n$.  
If $p\mid\Phi_{n}(2)$, then $\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=n$.

\emph{Proof.}  
Let $r:=\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$; then $r\mid n$.  Suppose $r<n$ and
write $n=rk$ with $k>1$.  Because $2^{\,r}\equiv 1\pmod p$ we have
\[
\frac{2^{\,n}-1}{2^{\,r}-1}=1+2^{\,r}+2^{2r}+\,\dots+\,2^{(k-1)r}
\equiv k \pmod p .
\]
Now $p\mid 2^{\,n}-1$ and $p\mid 2^{\,r}-1$, hence
$p$ divides the left-hand side and therefore $p\mid k$.
But $p\nmid r$ (since $r\mid p-1$), so $p\mid k$ implies
$p\mid n$, contradicting the hypothesis.  Hence $r=n$.\qed



\bigskip
\textbf{2.\;Existence of a primitive prime divisor}

\medskip\noindent
\textbf{Theorem 2.1.} (\emph{Elementary Bang, base $2$})  
For every integer $n>1$, $n\neq 6$, the number $2^{\,n}-1$ possesses a
primitive prime divisor.

\emph{Proof.}  
Let $\mathcal P$ be the set of odd primes dividing $\Phi_{n}(2)$.

\emph{Step 1.}  
There exists $p\in\mathcal P$ with $p\nmid n$.  
Indeed, if every $q\in\mathcal P$ divided $n$, then by Lemma 1.1
\[
\Phi_{n}(2)\le\prod_{q\mid n}q=\operatorname{rad}(n)\le n,
\]
contradicting Lemma 0.2.

\emph{Step 2.}  
Such a prime $p$ is primitive by Lemma 1.2, for
$\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=n$. \qed



\bigskip
\textbf{3.\;The exceptional exponent $n=6$}

\[
2^{\,6}-1=63=3^{2}\cdot 7,\qquad
\operatorname{ord}_{3}(2)=2,\qquad
\operatorname{ord}_{7}(2)=3,
\]
so no primitive prime divisor occurs.  This completes the proof of part (a).

\bigskip
\textbf{4.\;Proof of the corollary}

Assume, for a contradiction, that $n>1$ satisfies $n\mid 2^{\,n}-1$.  
Because $2^{\,n}-1$ is odd, $n$ is odd.  
Let $p$ be the smallest prime factor of $n$ and put
$\lambda=\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$.  
Then $\lambda\mid n$ and, by Fermat's little theorem, $\lambda\mid p-1<p$.  
Hence $\lambda$ has a prime factor $r<p$, but $r\mid\lambda\mid n$, contradicting the minimality of $p$.  
Therefore no $n>1$ satisfies $n\mid 2^{\,n}-1$.\qed

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  The original problem only required ruling out n | 2^{\,n}-1 via a simple “smallest prime factor” argument.  

•  The enhanced variant demands the construction of a primitive prime divisor of 2^{\,n}-1, essentially proving the special-case n=2 of Zsigmondy’s theorem without being allowed to quote it.  This forces the solver to invoke cyclotomic polynomials, analyse their pairwise gcd’s, and understand how multiplicative orders behave—tools that lie well beyond elementary modular arithmetic.

•  The exception n=6 must be detected and handled separately, adding a delicate “edge-case” analysis.

•  Part (b) then intertwines the primitive-divisor machinery with the classical minimal-prime-factor trick; two different strands of number-theoretic reasoning have to be coordinated.

•  Overall the solution requires several non-trivial lemmas, a deeper structural factorisation (2^{\,n}-1 via cyclotomic polynomials), the arithmetic of multiplicative orders, and a careful treatment of exceptional cases—making the task substantially harder and longer than both the original problem and the standard kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n$ be an integer with $n>1$.

(a)  (Elementary Bang-Zsigmondy theorem for the base $2$)  

 Except for the single exponent $n=6$, every $n$ admits a
primitive prime divisor; that is, there exists an odd prime
\[
p\mid 2^{\,n}-1 \qquad\text{with}\qquad \operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=n .
\]
Equivalently $n\mid p-1$, so $p\equiv 1\pmod n$ and therefore $p\ge n+1$.

 Give a proof \emph{using only}
 * elementary properties of multiplicative order and Euler's $\varphi$-function;  
 * Euler's theorem;  
 * the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma in the form  
\[
v_{p}(a^{\,m}-b^{\,m})=v_{p}(a-b)+v_{p}(m),
\qquad
\bigl(p\text{ odd},\,p\mid a-b,\,m\ge 1\bigr);
\]
 * the identities
\[
\frac{2^{\,n}-1}{2^{\,d}-1}=1+2^{\,d}+2^{2d}+\dots+2^{(k-1)d},
\qquad n=k\,d ,
\]
and
\[
2^{\,n}-1=\prod_{d\mid n}\Phi_{d}(2)\qquad(n\ge 1),
\]
where $\Phi_{d}$ denotes the $d$-th cyclotomic polynomial.

\emph{No analytic number theory, no algebraic number theory beyond the
above facts, and no use of Zsigmondy's original proof is permitted.}

(b)  Corollary  
 Show that no integer $n>1$ satisfies
\[
n\mid 2^{\,n}-1 .
\]
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, $v_{p}$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation and $\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$ the multiplicative order of $2$ modulo the (odd) prime $p$. 0. Two preliminary lemmata Lemma 0.1. Let $p$ be an odd prime and $m\ge 1$. Put \[ G_{p,m}:=\frac{2^{\,p m}-1}{2^{\,m}-1}=1+2^{\,m}+2^{2m}+\dots+2^{(p-1)m}. \] Then (i) $v_{p}(G_{p,m})=0$ unless $2^{\,m}\equiv 1\pmod p$; (ii) if $2^{\,m}\equiv 1\pmod p$ then $v_{p}(G_{p,m})=1$ and $G_{p,m}>p$. Proof. Write $2^{\,m}=1+kp$ with $k\in\mathbf Z$. Because $p\mid 2^{\,m}-1$, the Lifting-the-Exponent lemma gives \[ v_{p}(2^{\,p m}-1)=v_{p}(2^{\,m}-1)+v_{p}(p) =v_{p}(2^{\,m}-1)+1 . \] Subtracting $v_{p}(2^{\,m}-1)$ yields (i) and the equality $v_{p}(G_{p,m})=1$ in case $2^{\,m}\equiv1\pmod p$. Finally $G_{p,m}=\sum_{j=0}^{p-1}2^{jm}\ge\sum_{j=0}^{p-1}1=p$, and strict inequality holds because at least one summand exceeds $1$. \qed Lemma 0.2. (Universal lower bound) For every $n>1$, $n\neq6$ one has \[ \Phi_{n}(2)>n . \tag{0.1} \] Proof. Exactly as in the original write-up; the argument is unchanged and is reproduced in Appendix A for completeness. \qed 1. How often do primes dividing $n$ occur in $\Phi_{n}(2)$? Lemma 1.1. Let $p$ be an odd prime with $p\mid n$. Then \[ v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{n}(2)\bigr)\le 1 . \tag{1.1} \] (The bound is best possible: equality can indeed occur, for instance $(n,p)=(6,3)$ or $(20,5)$.) Proof. Write $n=p^{e}m$ with $e\ge1$ and $p\nmid m$. \emph{Step 1. Reduction to the first power of $p$.} Put \[ H:=\frac{2^{\,p^{e}m}-1}{2^{\,p^{e-1}m}-1} =1+2^{\,p^{e-1}m}+2^{2p^{e-1}m}+\dots+2^{(p-1)p^{e-1}m}. \] By Lemma 0.1 one has $v_{p}(H)\le1$. The cyclotomic factorisation \[ H =\prod_{k\mid m}\Phi_{p^{e}k}(2) \] implies \[ v_{p}(H)=\sum_{k\mid m}v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{p^{e}k}(2)\bigr). \] Because every summand is non-negative, each individual valuation is bounded by $1$; in particular \[ v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{n}(2)\bigr)=v_{p}\!\bigl(\Phi_{p^{e}m}(2)\bigr)\le1 . \] \qed 2. Existence of a primitive prime divisor Theorem 2.1 (Elementary Bang, base $2$). For every $n>1$, $n\neq6$, the number $2^{\,n}-1$ has a primitive prime divisor. Proof. Let $\mathcal P$ be the set of primes $q$ dividing $\Phi_{n}(2)$. Suppose, for contradiction, that \emph{every} $q\in\mathcal P$ satisfies $q\mid n$. By Lemma 1.1 one has $v_{q}\bigl(\Phi_{n}(2)\bigr)\le1$, so \[ \Phi_{n}(2) \le \prod_{q\mid n}q \le n . \tag{2.1} \] Inequality (0.1) contradicts (2.1) for all $n\neq6$; therefore some prime $p\in\mathcal P$ must satisfy $p\nmid n$. For such a prime $p$ we have $p\mid\Phi_{n}(2)$, hence $2^{\,n}\equiv1\pmod p$ and $2^{\,d}\not\equiv1\pmod p$ for every proper divisor $d\mid n$. Consequently $\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)=n$, so $p$ is primitive. \qed 3. The exceptional exponent $n=6$ \[ 2^{\,6}-1=63=3^{2}\!\cdot7,\qquad \operatorname{ord}_{3}(2)=2,\qquad \operatorname{ord}_{7}(2)=3; \] hence no primitive prime divisor occurs, and $n=6$ is indeed the only exception. 4. Proof of the corollary Assume, for contradiction, that $n>1$ satisfies $n\mid 2^{\,n}-1$. Because $2^{\,n}-1$ is odd, $n$ must also be odd. Let $p$ be the \emph{smallest} prime divisor of $n$. Since $p\mid n$ and $n\mid 2^{\,n}-1$, we have \[ 2^{\,n}\equiv 1\pmod p . \] Let $\lambda:=\operatorname{ord}_{p}(2)$. Then $\lambda$ divides $n$. On the other hand, by Fermat's little theorem $2^{\,p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p$, so $\lambda\mid p-1$ as well. Consequently \[ 1<\lambda\le p-1

1$ can satisfy $n\mid 2^{\,n}-1$. \qed Appendix A. Proof of Lemma 0.2 [The detailed three-step proof given in the original write-up is inserted here verbatim, establishing $\Phi_{n}(2)>n$ for all $n\neq6$.]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  The original problem only required ruling out n | 2^{\,n}-1 via a simple “smallest prime factor” argument.  

•  The enhanced variant demands the construction of a primitive prime divisor of 2^{\,n}-1, essentially proving the special-case n=2 of Zsigmondy’s theorem without being allowed to quote it.  This forces the solver to invoke cyclotomic polynomials, analyse their pairwise gcd’s, and understand how multiplicative orders behave—tools that lie well beyond elementary modular arithmetic.

•  The exception n=6 must be detected and handled separately, adding a delicate “edge-case” analysis.

•  Part (b) then intertwines the primitive-divisor machinery with the classical minimal-prime-factor trick; two different strands of number-theoretic reasoning have to be coordinated.

•  Overall the solution requires several non-trivial lemmas, a deeper structural factorisation (2^{\,n}-1 via cyclotomic polynomials), the arithmetic of multiplicative orders, and a careful treatment of exceptional cases—making the task substantially harder and longer than both the original problem and the standard kernel variant.
